sources_audited.note: The cited Census URL reached the official 2022 NAICS portal, but its client-rendered search result did not expose the 445131 definition in the reopened page text. The portal is appropriate for the classification claim, but the exact limited-line and exclusion wording could not be fully reverified from this citation alone.

The May 2024 OEWS table reports the cited occupation counts exactly for Food and Beverage Retailers (4451 and 4452 only), including 838,060 cashiers, 637,090 stockers and order fillers, 185,500 food preparation workers, 168,200 retail supervisors, and 132,220 fast-food and counter workers.

BLS lists checkout, customer-assistance, age-verification, pickup, stocking, and light-upkeep duties and projects cashier employment down 10 percent from 2024 to 2034, attributing the decline to technology including self-service checkout and increasing online sales.

The Census graphic reports 51 percent age 55 and older, 43 percent age 35 to 54, and 6 percent age 34 and under for 4.1 million responding owners of employer businesses, using the 2019 ABS with data year 2018.

The Census-sourced FRED series is monthly, nominal, not seasonally adjusted, and in millions of dollars; it reports 89,736 for December 2024 and 91,318 for December 2025. It supports the broad sales observations used around d5 but does not observe AI-savings pass-through or otherwise substantiate q.
USDA ERS reports real food-at-home spending up 1.8 percent in 2024 after a 2.6 percent decline in 2023 and real food-away-from-home spending up 0.4 percent in 2024, exactly as cited.

caveats: The official Census NAICS URL was reachable, but its reopened client-rendered page did not expose the 445131 definition text, so S1's exact wording and exclusions were only partially reverified.

The outsourced-provisioning lens is a severe but disclosed narrowing of a walk-in retail code; no cited source measures whether contracted recurring-account businesses exist at the estimated 1 to 8 percent frequency.

The labor-exposure proxy uses employment counts for combined NAICS 4451 and 4452 rather than task-time or wage weights for nonfuel convenience retailers, so a's 0.22 to 0.50 range remains judgment-heavy despite an explicit bridge.
S3 establishes substitution pressure for cashier work, not five-year implementation of the broader ordering, scheduling, forecasting, messaging, and invoice workflows embedded in rho.
The s5 bridge converts 2018 all-industry responding-owner age shares into a five-year external control-transfer probability without industry-specific observed sales or a firm denominator.
The q range is an honest estimate, but neither S1 nor S5 measures competitive pass-through of AI-related savings; S5 only supplies broad nominal food-and-beverage-store sales.

The d5 sources concern broad food-at-home, food-away-from-home, and food-and-beverage-store markets rather than recurring convenience-provisioning accounts; the packet discloses this bridge and its error directions.
The operator-retention primitive o is bounded judgment rather than observed channel-retention evidence, and S1/S3 support only classification and task examples rather than the numeric range.
summary: Mechanics pass and all six primitive-cited sources were reopened. The record is useful and unusually explicit about its narrow service lens, missing firm-count anchor, broad proxies, and judgmental estimates. S2, S3, S4, and S6 support their stated claims; S1 could not be fully reverified from its client-rendered citation, and S5 supports broad nominal sales but not q. These issues are caveats rather than material findings because correcting or downgrading the affected researched inputs would not change the absent base tier or the EVIDENCE_FIRST action while n remains MISSING.
